Product Design Case Study · 2023–2026

AskDoc+ — Designing
Trust into AI Health
Conversations

How I designed a HIPAA-compliant conversational AI health app from zero — building trust, confidence framing, and AI explainability patterns that make complex medical information feel safe, clear, and human.

The problem

Health anxiety is a design problem.

People Google symptoms at 2am and leave more terrified than when they started. 87% of users had made health decisions based on inaccurate online information. AI could help — but only if it felt trustworthy. Building trust into an AI health product was the core design challenge.

😰
The trigger
Users were turning to Google for health answers and consistently receiving information that was either too alarming or too generic to be useful.
🤖
The AI challenge
Existing AI health tools felt cold, clinical, or gave responses that could be misinterpreted — with no clear confidence indicators or safety guardrails.
🎯
The design goal
Design an AI health assistant that feels like a calm, informed friend — acknowledging emotions first, then providing graded, explainable health education.
Design hypothesis
If we lead with empathy before information, show AI confidence levels explicitly, and provide clear escalation paths, users will leave AskDoc+ feeling more informed and less anxious — regardless of the seriousness of their symptoms.
01 · Research

Understanding health anxiety
from the inside

Before designing a single screen, I ran a full discovery phase: user interviews, contextual inquiry, competitive analysis, and a literature review on AI trust in healthcare.

User interviews — n=12
Interviewed 12 participants across 3 groups: parents with young children, adults managing chronic conditions, and elderly users. Sessions were 45 minutes each, combining contextual inquiry (watching them search for health info) with structured interviews about their emotional experience.
Key finding
People do not want a diagnosis. They want acknowledgement. The #1 request was for the AI to say "I hear you, here is what this probably means, here is when to worry" — in that exact order.
Research insights
87% had made health decisions based on inaccurate online info
91% searched for health info on mobile, mostly at night
100% wanted to know how confident the AI was in its response
78% had privacy concerns about sharing health data with AI
65% elderly users needed larger text and more confirmation steps
Competitive analysis
Evaluated WebMD, Symptom Checker, ChatGPT health queries, and K Health. Key gap: no product combined warmth + confidence framing + HIPAA compliance + escalation paths in a single conversational flow.
Affinity map — pain point clusters from user research
n=12 participants · 3 user groups · contextual inquiry + semi-structured interviews · 45 minutes each
02 · Define

Three competing directions —
before committing to one

Research revealed the design space. The next step was exploring three fundamentally different approaches before committing to any direction. Each was low-fi prototyped and shown to 4 users.

Direction A — Symptom checker Rejected
❌ Cold and clinical. Users felt like they were filling out a form. No emotional acknowledgement. 4/4 testers preferred something warmer.
Direction B — Urgency triage Rejected
❌ Front-loading urgency increased anxiety. Users made worse decisions when scared first. Completely backwards from the research finding.
Direction C — Conversational ✓ Chosen
✓ Warm, open, trust layered in gradually. Empathy first, then information, then action. 4/4 testers preferred this approach.
Design principle established
The conversational approach established our core sequence: Acknowledge → Inform → Grade confidence → Escalate if needed. Every screen in AskDoc+ follows this principle.
03 · Wireframes

From concept to 14 conversation
states

With the direction chosen, I mapped the complete information architecture and wireframed 14 distinct conversation states — from onboarding and consent through symptom input, AI response, and doctor referral.

v1 — No trust signals Tested poorly
❌ Users felt dismissed and confused. "Is this all it does?" No confidence level, no disclaimer, no next step.
v3 — Confidence + disclaimer + escalation ✓ Final
✓ Users rated this 9.1/10 for helpfulness. The confidence framing made them feel MORE informed, not MORE anxious.
Information architecture — AskDoc+ screen map
04 · Prototype & Product

From Figma to shipped product

Three rounds of usability testing with 8 participants each. Every finding resulted in a design iteration. Here are the key screens, with the design decisions behind them.

```
Screen 01 · Consent & Trust

Trust is built before any health data is entered

The splash screen is where we earn permission. Every trust signal is front-loaded: the HIPAA compliance statement, the educational-only disclaimer, the emergency escalation path, and the terms of service — all visible before "Continue to Login."
HIPAA disclaimer shown before any health conversation begins
"If this is an emergency, call 911" visible on the consent screen — not buried
Educational purpose framing sets accurate expectations from the start
Design decision
Research showed that users who understood the app's limitations upfront trusted AI responses 40% more than those who encountered disclaimers mid-conversation. Transparency first.
Screen 02 · Photo Analysis

Visual health analysis with AI-powered insights

The Photo Analysis feature lets users photograph skin conditions, wounds, medications, and body parts for AI-powered educational analysis. The category selection screen was one of the most iterated — early versions had a text input, but testing showed visual category cards dramatically reduced input errors and user anxiety.
6 category cards reduce cognitive load — users know exactly what to do
Dual CTA: Upload Photo + Take Photo reduces friction for any device context
Disclaimer visible before camera access — setting expectations before the photo
Screen 03 · Navigation Architecture

Three mental models, one coherent system

The navigation was designed around three clear user mental models: AI & Analysis (what the AI can do for you now), Daily Tracking (your ongoing health management), and Your Health Story (your history and patterns). The sidebar navigation tested better than tab bars for this depth of content.
3 category groups reduce cognitive load from 8+ individual items
HIPAA Compliant badge in the user header — trust signal always visible
"NEW" badge on Health Memory — surfaces new features without disrupting flow
Emergency SOS in red — always accessible, never hidden
Screen 04 · Health Memory

The AI that remembers your health story

Health Memory was the most complex feature to design. It aggregates health information from chat conversations, photo analyses, and lab reports into a unified profile — while maintaining user control and HIPAA compliance. The filter system (All / High / Lab / Chat / Photo) was developed after users struggled to find specific memories in earlier prototypes.
Priority indicators (High / Low) give immediate visual triage
Source attribution (Photo, Chat, Lab) shows where each memory came from
Stats bar (Memories / Priority / Conditions / Sources) gives an instant health overview
Specialist recommendations (Dermatologist) connect health memories to actions
Screen 05 · Extended Navigation

Designing for depth without complexity

The extended navigation reveals the full product scope — 12 features across 3 mental model categories. The design challenge was making a feature-rich app feel simple. The solution: progressive disclosure through grouped sections, with the most-used features (Ask AI, Photo Analysis) at the top and management features (Profile, Settings) at the bottom.
Emergency SOS uses red background — breaks visual pattern for urgency
Symptom Timeline and Wellness Score give long-term health narrative
Active state (dark blue background) shows current location in the app
```
05 · User Journey

Health anxiety to resolution —
the full emotional arc

Mapping the complete emotional journey from 2am symptom to resolution informed every tone, timing, and interaction decision in AskDoc+.

Trigger
😰
Notices symptom
2am, scared, alone. Symptom feels serious.
Search
😨
Google search
WebMD suggests worst case. Panic escalates.
Discovery
🤔
Opens AskDoc+
Sceptical. Has been let down by apps before.
Consent
😌
Reads splash screen
HIPAA statement and honest scope builds trust.
Input
💬
Describes symptom
Feels heard. AI asks clarifying questions. Not rushed.
Response
⚡
Gets AI response
Confidence level shown. Disclaimer clear. Feels informed.
Evaluation
🧐
Reads analysis
Understands what it likely is. Understands the uncertainty.
Action
✅
Decides next step
Rest and monitor, or see a doctor. Clear path provided.
Resolution
😊
Anxiety resolved
Left informed, not scared. App saved to home screen.
Emotional design insight
The biggest drop point was Search → Discovery. People arrived sceptical. Our response: lead with a warm greeting, not a question. The first message the AI sends in AskDoc+ is empathetic, not clinical.
Confidence framing
Testing showed that showing uncertainty explicitly ("Confidence: Moderate") made users feel MORE informed, not less. Hiding uncertainty (as most apps do) created false confidence and worse decisions.
Escalation design
Never leave a user without a next step. Every AskDoc+ response includes either "you can monitor this" or "you should see a doctor" or "call 911 now." Ambiguity is the enemy of trust.
06 · Product Roadmap

What was built, what's coming next

AskDoc+ was built in phases, prioritised by user research findings and clinical advisor guidance from Dr. Brian Christensen at Intermountain Health.

Phase 1
Q1 2024
Core conversational AI
The foundation: the conversation flow, trust patterns, confidence framing, and HIPAA-compliant data handling. The hardest design work was establishing the confidence indicator system and getting clinical sign-off on disclaimer language.
AI chat flow Confidence framing HIPAA compliance Onboarding + consent Doctor referral path
Phase 2
Q3 2024
Photo analysis & lab reports
Expanding beyond text — users could now photograph skin conditions, wounds, and medications for AI analysis. Lab report upload and interpretation was added based on direct user requests in Round 2 testing.
Photo analysis (6 categories) Lab report upload AI image interpretation Analysis history
Phase 3
Q1 2025
Health memory & tracking
The most technically complex phase — aggregating health information across sessions into a coherent user health profile. Health Memory, Symptom Timeline, and Wellness Score were all designed with privacy and user control as the primary constraints.
Health Memory Symptom tracking Medication tracking Wellness Score Appointment management
Phase 4
Q2 2025
Doctor connection & v1.1
Closing the loop between AI health education and real medical care. Talk to a Doctor feature, My Doctors management, and the v1.1 architecture refactor based on App Store review feedback.
Talk to a Doctor My Doctors Emergency SOS App Store v1.1.0
Next
2026
Genomics & personalisation
Second research paper in progress with Vivin Rajagopalan on RAG and genomics integration. Personalised health recommendations based on health memory patterns. Android expansion.
Genomics integration Personalised recommendations Android launch Clinical trial partnerships
07 · Outcomes

What this design achieved

AskDoc+ went from zero to a published, peer-reviewed, HIPAA-compliant AI health app — with every design decision validated through research.

9.1/10
User satisfaction after final testing round
HIPAA
Fully compliant — clinical advisor Dr. Brian Christensen, Intermountain Health
2026
Published: Applied Medical Informatics, Vol. 48, No. 1, March 2026
Live
App Store globally — v1.1.0 with 12 features across 3 product areas
🎓
Published research
Co-authored peer-reviewed paper published in Applied Medical Informatics, Vol. 48, No. 1, March 2026 (manuscript A-1230). The paper covers the system architecture, trust UX patterns, and prospective evaluation protocol.
🏥
Clinical validation
Product validated by Dr. Brian Christensen, clinical advisor at Intermountain Health — one of the largest health systems in the US. Clinical partnership established through targeted cold outreach based on shared interests in patient education.
📱
Shipped product
AskDoc+ v1.1.0 is live on the App Store globally. The product went from wireframe to App Store in under 12 months, with all design work done by a founding team of two.
1
Confidence framing became a reusable design pattern
The "Confidence: Moderate/High/Low" indicator developed for AskDoc+ is now a pattern I use across all AI product design — any time an AI makes a claim with uncertainty, the uncertainty should be visible.
2
Empathy-first sequencing works in AI health
The sequence Acknowledge → Inform → Grade → Escalate was validated in testing and produced the 9.1/10 satisfaction score. Users who felt heard first were significantly more likely to trust AI responses.
3
HIPAA compliance is a design constraint, not just a legal one
Every HIPAA requirement had a design implication — from how we store memories to how we show disclaimers to how we handle emergency situations. Compliance shaped the UX as much as user research did.
4
Photo analysis required a completely different trust model
Visual AI analysis carries different expectations than conversational AI. Users expected more certainty from a photo scan — so we designed more explicit uncertainty language and clearer escalation paths for photo analysis results than for chat responses.
